Curriculum → AI Engineer learning path → Deep learning → CNNs and computer vision

Lesson 2 of 3

CNNs and computer vision

Convolutional neural networks for image classification, object detection, and segmentation tasks.

30-40 min AI/ML flow Deep learning 4 guided stops

Study map

What this lesson helps you explain

CNNs are the foundation of computer vision systems deployed in autonomous vehicles, medical imaging, content moderation, and manufacturing quality control.

Start here Frame the trade-off before diving into architecture. 1. Convolution and pooling Filters detect edges, textures, and increasingly abstract patterns in deeper layers2. Modern architectures Skip connections in ResNet solve vanishing gradients in very deep networks3. Applied computer vision Transfer learning from ImageNet-pretrained models saves weeks of training

Guided walkthrough

Move through the lesson in a clear interview order

4 steps

Start with the framing, walk the concept in order, then use the right rail for prompts, pitfalls, and visual anchors.

1. Interview framing CNNs are the foundation of computer vision systems deployed in autonomous vehicles, medical imaging, content moderation, and manufacturing quality control. 2. Convolution and pooling Filters detect edges, textures, and increasingly abstract patterns in deeper layers3. Modern architectures Skip connections in ResNet solve vanishing gradients in very deep networks4. Applied computer vision Transfer learning from ImageNet-pretrained models saves weeks of training
1

Start with this framing

Interview framing

CNNs are the foundation of computer vision systems deployed in autonomous vehicles, medical imaging, content moderation, and manufacturing quality control.

What to say first

  • Can explain how a convolutional layer differs from a fully connected layer.
  • Understands transfer learning and when to freeze versus fine-tune layers.
  • Knows the tradeoffs between YOLO and Faster R-CNN for detection tasks.
2

Study section

Convolution and pooling

Convolutional layers learn local spatial features; pooling reduces spatial dimensions while preserving important signals.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Filters detect edges, textures, and increasingly abstract patterns in deeper layers
  • Stride and padding control output dimensions
  • Max pooling provides translation invariance; average pooling preserves more spatial info

Code example

Build a CNN for image classification

import torch.nn as nn

class CNN(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, num_classes=10):
        super().__init__()
        self.features = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Conv2d(3, 32, kernel_size=3, padding=1),  # 32x32 -> 32x32
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.MaxPool2d(2),                              # 32x32 -> 16x16
            nn.Conv2d(32, 64, kernel_size=3, padding=1), # 16x16 -> 16x16
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.MaxPool2d(2),                              # 16x16 -> 8x8
        )
        self.classifier = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Flatten(),
            nn.Linear(64 * 8 * 8, 128),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(128, num_classes),
        )

    def forward(self, x):
        x = self.features(x)
        return self.classifier(x)

model = CNN(num_classes=10)
print(f"Parameters: {sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters()):,}")
3

Study section

Modern architectures

ResNet, EfficientNet, and Vision Transformers (ViT) represent the evolution from hand-crafted to learned architectures.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Skip connections in ResNet solve vanishing gradients in very deep networks
  • EfficientNet uses compound scaling to balance depth, width, and resolution
  • Vision Transformers apply self-attention to image patches for global context
4

Study section

Applied computer vision

Object detection (YOLO, Faster R-CNN), semantic segmentation (U-Net), and transfer learning enable practical vision systems.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Transfer learning from ImageNet-pretrained models saves weeks of training
  • Data augmentation (flips, crops, color jitter) regularizes on small datasets
  • Real-time inference requires model optimization: quantization, pruning, TensorRT

Checklist

Things to say clearly

  • Can explain how a convolutional layer differs from a fully connected layer.
  • Understands transfer learning and when to freeze versus fine-tune layers.
  • Knows the tradeoffs between YOLO and Faster R-CNN for detection tasks.

Common pitfalls

What to avoid

  • Training from scratch on small datasets instead of leveraging pretrained models.
  • Ignoring data augmentation which is often more impactful than architecture changes.
  • Not considering inference latency requirements when choosing architectures.

Interview prompts

Practice aloud

  • How do skip connections help train deeper networks?
  • When would you use a Vision Transformer instead of a CNN?
  • Explain the tradeoff between model accuracy and inference speed in production.

Related topics

Keep the thread going

AI study companion

Go beyond the overview with practical examples

Use the highlights below to study the lesson more deeply, then walk through runnable examples and system-design drills.

1 code example 1 design drill

What to master

Study this in your own words

  • Can explain how a convolutional layer differs from a fully connected layer.
  • Understands transfer learning and when to freeze versus fine-tune layers.
  • Knows the tradeoffs between YOLO and Faster R-CNN for detection tasks.

Production lens

Failure modes to call out

  • Training from scratch on small datasets instead of leveraging pretrained models.
  • Ignoring data augmentation which is often more impactful than architecture changes.
  • Not considering inference latency requirements when choosing architectures.

Practice aloud

Questions worth rehearsing

  • How do skip connections help train deeper networks?
  • When would you use a Vision Transformer instead of a CNN?
  • Explain the tradeoff between model accuracy and inference speed in production.

Code example

Build and analyze a CNN for MNIST

intermediate coding

Build a CNN in PyTorch for MNIST digit classification. Visualize what the first convolutional layer learns by examining filter weights.

What to notice

  • MNIST images are 1x28x28 (grayscale)
  • After two MaxPool2d(2,2) layers: 28->14->7
  • First conv layer filters often learn edge detectors

Expected outcome

Model achieves >98% accuracy; first layer filters show edge-like patterns

Starter code 
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.optim as optim

class SimpleCNN(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        # TODO: Define layers
        # Conv2d -> ReLU -> MaxPool -> Conv2d -> ReLU -> MaxPool -> Flatten -> Linear
        pass

    def forward(self, x):
        # TODO: Implement forward pass
        pass

# TODO: Train for 3 epochs on MNIST
# TODO: Print first conv layer filter shapes and norms
Reference solution 
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class SimpleCNN(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.conv1 = nn.Conv2d(1, 16, kernel_size=3, padding=1)
        self.conv2 = nn.Conv2d(16, 32, kernel_size=3, padding=1)
        self.pool = nn.MaxPool2d(2, 2)
        self.fc1 = nn.Linear(32 * 7 * 7, 128)
        self.fc2 = nn.Linear(128, 10)
        self.relu = nn.ReLU()

    def forward(self, x):
        x = self.pool(self.relu(self.conv1(x)))
        x = self.pool(self.relu(self.conv2(x)))
        x = x.view(-1, 32 * 7 * 7)
        x = self.relu(self.fc1(x))
        return self.fc2(x)

Design drill

Transfer learning strategy design

beginner

Given a new task (classifying 500 medical X-ray images into 5 categories), design a transfer learning strategy. Decide which layers to freeze, what to fine-tune, and what data augmentation to apply.

Use these prompts to deepen the study pass

  • Which pretrained model would you choose and why (ResNet50, EfficientNet, ViT)?
  • How many layers would you freeze vs fine-tune given only 500 images?
  • What data augmentation is appropriate for medical images (vs general photos)?
  • How would you handle class imbalance if some categories have fewer examples?

Interactive practice lab

Write, save, and move through the mock interview

Answers stay in your browser so you can come back later.

0/3 saved Step 1/3: Explain the concept

PRACTICE GUIDE

Explain the concept Demonstrate deep understanding by explaining the topic clearly, covering intuition, algorithms, and practical applications.

Explain the concept

Explain "CNNs and computer vision" as if teaching a senior engineer who is new to AI/ML. Cover the core intuition, when and why you would use it, and the key algorithms or techniques involved.

Goal

Demonstrate deep understanding by explaining the topic clearly, covering intuition, algorithms, and practical applications.

Guardrails

  • How do skip connections help train deeper networks?
  • Convolution and pooling
  • Modern architectures
  • Can explain how a convolutional layer differs from a fully connected layer.

Suggested Structure

  • Core intuition
  • Key algorithms or techniques
  • When to apply
  • Common variants
